Speaker B means that the slow progress is not due to inefficiency, laziness, or a mistake, but rather because the task itself requires a series of necessary steps that take time. By saying "It's a process," B is asking A to be patient and accept that things cannot be rushed, as the timeline is dictated by the nature of the work, not by anyone's choices. It is a way of saying, "This is how it works, and we just have to go through the steps."

Speaker B means that A's outrage or wish that things were different is unrealistic. B is pointing out that suffering is not an accidental or fixable flaw of war, but rather its very essence. By saying "War always involves suffering," B is telling A to accept this harsh reality because it is a fundamental, unchangeable truth about armed conflict. The reply implies that complaining or hoping for a war without suffering is pointless, because suffering is what defines war in the first place.

Speaker B means that A's failure to finish was not due to a personal mistake or lack of effort, but simply because time is a fixed, unchangeable limit that everyone must respect. By stating "Time is time," B is essentially saying that time behaves exactly as it always does-it passes, it runs out, and no one can control or stretch it. The reply is a way of telling A to accept the outcome because it was inevitable given the constraint of time, and there is no point in dwelling on it. It is a resigned, matter-of-fact acknowledgment that time cannot be bargained with.

Speaker B means that the children's loud and messy behavior is completely normal and expected for their age. By saying "Kids are kids," B is telling A not to be upset or surprised, because this is simply how children naturally behave. The reply implies that A should accept the situation with tolerance and patience, rather than expecting children to act like well-behaved adults. It is a way of saying, "This is just what kids do, so there's no point in complaining about it."

Speaker B means that A's feelings or lack of motivation are not a valid excuse for breaking a commitment. B is telling A that they have a moral obligation to follow through on what they promised, regardless of whether they currently "feel like" doing it. The reply emphasizes that keeping promises is a matter of principle and integrity, not convenience or mood. It is a firm reminder that A should act responsibly and honor their word, even when it is inconvenient or unappealing.

Speaker B means that the complicated situation A is experiencing is not unusual or unique-it is simply the kind of difficulty that happens in life from time to time. By saying "It's just one of those situations," B is telling A not to overthink it or expect a simple solution, because some situations are inherently messy and complex. The reply implies that A should accept the difficulty as a normal part of life rather than trying to fight against it or change it. It is a way of saying, "These things happen, and you just have to deal with them."

Speaker B means that A's suggestion to postpone the work is not acceptable. B is emphasizing that deadlines are not optional suggestions-they are firm commitments that must be honored. By saying "Deadlines need to be respected," B is telling A that they have a responsibility to complete the work on time, regardless of whether they feel like doing it later. The reply implies that ignoring deadlines would be unprofessional and irresponsible, and that A should prioritize meeting the deadline rather than trying to avoid it.

Speaker B means that A's expectation that things should not be so hard is unrealistic. B is pointing out that difficulty is a normal and frequent part of human existence, not an exception or a mistake. By saying "Life is often difficult," B is telling A to accept this reality rather than resist it or complain about it. The reply implies that A should adjust their expectations and understand that challenges and hardships are simply part of the human experience, so it is better to accept them and move forward rather than dwell on how things "should" be.

Speaker B means that A's disappointment in their friends is unnecessary because this is just how friends naturally are. By saying "Friends are friends," B is telling A to accept their friends as they are, flaws and all, rather than expecting them to be perfect or always meet A's expectations. The reply implies that friendship involves accepting people for who they are, even when they fall short, and that A should be more tolerant and forgiving. It is a way of saying, "This is what friends are like, so don't be surprised or upset-just accept it."

Speaker B means that the loud and emotional behavior of football supporters is nothing unusual or surprising—it is simply typical for that group of people. By saying "Football supporters tend to behave like that," B is telling A not to be surprised or annoyed, because this is just how football fans naturally act in that environment. The reply implies that A should accept the behavior as normal and expected, rather than questioning it or expecting it to change. It is a way of saying, "This is just what they do, so you might as well get used to it."

Speaker B means that A cannot escape the consequences of the bet just because it was made "for fun." By saying "A bet is a bet," B is emphasizing that once a bet is made, it creates a binding obligation that must be honored, regardless of A's feelings or excuses. The reply implies that there is a principle of fairness and integrity at stake-if you agree to a bet, you must follow through on the outcome, or your word becomes meaningless. B is telling A to take responsibility and do what they agreed to do, because that is simply how bets work.

Speaker B means that they do not have a clear or certain answer to A's question about why "they" did something. By saying "It's hard to say," B is indicating that the reason is not obvious, that they lack enough information, or that the situation is too complex to give a simple explanation. The reply suggests that B does not want to guess or speculate, and it may also imply that there could be multiple possible reasons, none of which are clear. It is a neutral, honest response that acknowledges uncertainty rather than providing a definitive answer.

Speaker B means that A cannot simply ignore or skip part of the contract just because they feel like it. By saying "A contract is a contract," B is emphasizing that contracts are legally and morally binding documents that must be honored in their entirety. The reply implies that there is a principle of integrity, trust, and legality at stake-if you sign a contract, you are obligated to follow all its terms, not just the ones you like. B is telling A to take their commitment seriously and fulfill their obligations, because that is simply how contracts work and what it means to be a responsible party to an agreement.

Speaker B means that the decision that A finds cold or heartless is simply how business operates. By saying "Business is business," B is emphasizing that in the world of commerce and organizations, decisions are often made based on logic, efficiency, and practical considerations, rather than emotions or personal feelings. The reply implies that A should accept the decision and not take it personally, because this is the nature of business-it prioritizes results over sentiment. B is telling A to be realistic and understand that business rules are different from personal relationships, and that acting with "the human side" in mind is not always how things work in that context.

Speaker B means that the immature behavior A is complaining about is nothing unusual or surprising—it is simply typical for boys. The reply implies that A should accept the behavior as normal and expected, rather than getting frustrated or trying to change it. It is a way of saying, "This is just what they do, so you might as well get used to it and be more tolerant."

Syntactically slightly awkward in English, but this sentiment is often expressed. Expresses an inevitability of suffering in war but without specific regard to human behavior. Certainly doesn’t imply rule of law. The tone of speaker B may either be resigned or even derisive if there is a political context to the conversation

Has the cadence of a common expression/aphorism but no such expression exists in English. The response implies an unwillingness to make an exception to a concrete rule. The situation wasn’t inevitable as speaker A could have managed their time better, but the result is final.

Awkward; a more common phrasing would be “kids will be kids,” which more firmly expresses inevitability. There is implied inevitability to their behavior and as such forgiveness or tolerance towards them.
The situation isn’t inevitable or unresolvable, but a favorable outcome for speaker A would require social principles to be broken. There is thus a social obligation to abide by such principles.

Too vague to determine if there is a social component to this, but regardless there isn’t necessarily an implication that the situation is inevitable or unchangeable, only difficult. Implies a mutual understanding/familiarity of this sort of situation between the speakers

Similar to the “promises” one. The situation isn’t inherently unchangeable but not respecting the deadline would contravene a rule/expectation. However such obligation seems less social in nature than the obligation to obey a promise. It seems like there are fixed rules in place that do not allow for flexibility.

The sentiment is common but the syntax is slightly robotic. Again too vague to determine if there is a social dynamic at play, but speaker B is not expressing any sort of obligation to speaker A, only an aphoristic acknowledgement of the difficult of their situation.

This one is difficult as the word “friends” obviously has a positive connotation but the expression “___ will be ____” implies a mutually understood, somewhat negative characteristic or behavior associated with a group of people, which isn’t as immediate as in the phrase “kids will be kids” or “boys will be boys”. In any case, the speaker is implying that this behavior is natural and some degree of tolerance for it is expected. Also perhaps this phrase doesn’t work because there is no specific demographic/group of people associated with the word “friends” and thus no specific behaviors associated with it.

Either speaker B is suggesting that the decision is irreversible because it is supported by many other people, or that the situation isn’t perceived as poorly by others, so there isn’t as much an exigent need to change the situation as speaker A may believe.
Such behavior is normal and inevitable for this group, and in the absence of any specific condemnation from speaker B there is an implied tolerance or at least resignation to the behavior.
Speaker B is expressing (either honestly or dishonestly) that they understood speaker A’s bet as a sincerely placed bet and thus they are subject to the obligation to uphold the terms of the bet.

Speaker B is expressing a difficulty in accounting for the behavior of this 3rd person. There is otherwise no strong implication inherent to this phrase alone. Depending on the context, it can be positive or negative. Their behavior is probably not normal or to be expected, hence the difficultly in accounting for it.
Similar to the “bet” prompt. Speaker B is requiring speaker A to uphold the terms of the contract.

Speaker B implies that it is futile to condemn business practices as inhumane because business practices are impersonal by nature. Such an expression is probably not resigned as the previous “war” aphorism is, and is likely being used in order to ethically justify the business practice in question.

The expression “boys will be boys” because of its sing-songy cadence implies a degree of tolerance/forgiveness of the behavior. However, here speaker B expresses a behavioral norm but not necessarily with the same degree of tolerance. In fact, there is perhaps an exasperation in speaker B’s tone.
